item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
The image that was used in this article could not be found under any source as cc-compliant. Thus, it is assumed that the image is non-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0) and has been removed.

EDIT: The image that was used in this article has been replaced with an image that's cc-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0) and is sourced as follows:

Title: 1673-Active-new.png
Author(s): Tomas Gunnarsson (Genusfotografen), Engin_Akyurt, Rolle Ruhland, Elogee FishTruck
Release year: 2020
Note: Created by Elogee FishTruck utilizing the images below in the final image.

Source: Wikimedia Commons
License: CC BY-SA 4.0
Title: Extended arm.jpg
Author: Tomas Gunnarsson (Genusfotografen)

Source: Needpix
License: Public Domain
Title: Male Arm Nov
Author: Engin_Akyurt

Source: Flickr
License: CC BY 2.0
Title: :::GRAVEYARD IN THE DARK:::
Author: Rolle Ruhland

item.title: JStaff Post - Open:
item.content:encoded:
I was going to make a smart ass comment, but I think I should be entirely clear here:

Soultear. This is shit-crit. It's a pathetic hand wave that addresses nothing, gives no direction, and is incredibly vitriolic. Do not do this. No one is going to respect your feedback if you can't back it up.

Again do not do this. This is in direct violation of the site's criticism policy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: why's it bad though. You've just made an assertion that the writing is bad, and that you downvoted on this, without any real reason for why it's bad.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It sucks so much that I won't even bother pointing out each specific mistake.

This quote is so flawed I'm not gonna tell you why it's flawed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very helpful :P

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I wanted to say the writing is bad, but it isn't bad, it's worse than bad: The writing of this article sucks. It sucks so much that I won't even bother pointing out each specific mistake. This needs a total makeover, if only to correct the awful mistakes (It could also endure some brushing up, it feels outdated, like if the general quality of the site got better and better with time and this scip was just left behind). Downvoted

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it. It's creepy, but a little sad.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Anyone else reminded of the 'Helping Hands' from The Labyrinth?

item.title: Re: excessive containment
item.content:encoded: It is a public cemetery (and a historical landmark). You can't put an electric fence around it and not be noticed.

item.title: excessive containment
item.content:encoded:
I'm downvoting because the containment procedures seem waaaay too extreme.

This is a safe SCP, if no ones goes in nothing will happen. So a decent electric fence would do most of the work.

The perimeter wall of SCP-1673 is 3.1km, with a guard every 20m. Which means the foundation have 157 guards permanently assigned to stop some curious teens climbing over a wall.

And given that we have 157 guards, each of which are within shouting distance of each other, I'm not sure what an extra 6 guys patrolling are for?

This only makes any sense if this is actually something seriously hazardous and 05 are covering up.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Late but adjusted for this to make it more obvious they want to be helpful

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: How exactly do they know theyre trying to help? If a hand went to knock some dust off my pant leg, but accidentaly applied enough force to break my leg in the process i wouldnt jump to the conclusion that it was helping. The same goes for trying to tie my shoe and crushing my foot.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it. Simple, but effective, kind of like 1471.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's just trying to help. But OH NOES IT KILLS PEOPLE

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It was created in July 2012.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Something tells me that if this was created today, it would get a zillion downvotes.

But maybe that's just me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Foundation SOP runs more towards the deployment of Mobile Easement and Ablation Tools. Some variation of these cheap, disposable, and eminently versatile items has been employed by every combat force in the modern world. Every soldier is happy to have a ready supply of M.E.A.T. "shields". The Foundation even has their own variation, for exclusive in-organization use, D-Class.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: … And the Telekill katanas.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: …They're stored with our Lifeforce-Drainage guns.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: …probably not, no.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm with Carn. Also, doesn't the Foundation have things like kinetic shock absorber armor yet?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think an incident report would do wonder for this scp

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I approve. Much cleaner and easier to read.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fixed them redundancies, how d'ya like it now?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The beginning really threw me off. Way too many redundancies for me to enjoy.

SCP-1673 is to be surrounded by a perimeter completely surrounding the area at a distance of 500 meters. This area is to be patrolled by 3 teams of 2 guards patrolling the perimeter at all times. This perimeter is to have a 1.5 meter concrete wall surrounding it, with security personnel stationed every 20 meters.

In 3 sentences, you use the words "surround", "perimeter", and "patrol" 2-3 times each. Often in the same sentence. If you ask me, that's just sloppy writing. Heck, I half expected the SCP to require some sort of redundancy in describing it after reading the containment procedures.

The concept isn't terrible, but that first bit really threw me off. Also, why are we terminating civilians? I don't think I've ever seen that before. It'd make sense if it had some sort of memetic or biological contagion, but it don't.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think this is a good article, but the quickness that this received + votes compared to the heinous, evil scrutiny other also-worthy entries get over inane minutiae is exactly why I stopped logging in to this site and stopped voting. Heinous.

This comment made me laugh out loud.

If anything, standards for upvoted articles get too lax sometimes. We're a creative writing site. We're supposed to subject each others' work to intense scrutiny. That's how we get better.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
These limbs will attempt to perform small actions that are beneficial to the person, including tying shoelaces, adjusting clothing, brushing off dust from the persons clothing, and attempting to give the person a massage.

This line is where I upvoted.

The execution on the SCP is… just alright. But the idea is very good, and it comes through enough that it deserves a +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Could not agree more. The article is riddled with repetitions and redundancies like this even though the guides specifically mention avoiding them. It just makes the whole article, which isn't a bad concept, feel dumb.

Hopefully this isn't burried so that it can be fixed by the author or someone skilled at amending composition probelms.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: "oh no, something I didn't like accrued a heinous amount of internet points"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It just looks brownish grey to me. It seems fine.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well, yes. You know that, I know that, Roget knows that. Dirt doesn't know that. Dirt knows "Man dirty, clean him!" The dirt may apply 0.01% of its possible force. It may apply 99% of its possible force. You don't know what it will do, and neither does it.

Cause it's fuckin' DIRT.

That was my post to Hyenaboy up above, regarding the difference in force needed to brush a guy clean and kill the hell out of him. I was going to post down here why I like this SCP… And realized that pretty much sums it up.

Although I too would like to point out that soil is primarily made up of organic compounds; perhaps the line should read "testing has shown that they contain no human biological material whatsoever"?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well, yes. You know that, I know that, Roget knows that. Dirt doesn't know that. Dirt knows "Man dirty, clean him!" The dirt may apply 0.01% of its possible force. It may apply 99% of its possible force. You don't know what it will do, and neither does it.

Cause it's fuckin' DIRT.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's not just pleasing people in chat. It's pleasing people on the SITE. Certain people seem to get a lot of positive votes because, typically, their writing seems to fall in line with what the majority of this site seems to like. Sometimes that happens instantly, sometimes it takes a few days. Certain people get a lot of downvotes because they write very, very well, and could make countless tales, but their writing is so prosaic and verbose it completely destroys any clinical tone which is what we strive for with the main SCP listings. And of course, some get downvoted into oblivion because they're horrible.

That said, a lot of it does come down to personal choice. Take my own products: I personally think that SCP-1320 is better than SCP-767, but 767 has a higher rating. Why? It clicks more with more people, that's all. That's how it is with every entry on this wiki.

I can't even say how many things I've seen on this site where half the discussion is posts saying how glorious it is, and the other half is saying how the universe is forever tainted by it having existed. But I CAN say that the community on this site is a passionate one, and a lot of folks tend to either hang out in IRC, or watch the "recently posted" page and Announcement threads like a hawk. That means a lot of new things get pounced on very quickly, and when the majority of people on the site think along the same lines…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You're not convincing me. The amount of strength to murder someone is not inherent in brushing dirt from someone's clothing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Imagine a 3 year old child's mentality. Imagine a retarded down's-syndrome child's mentality.

And now, in my head, these are the lonely hands of retarded children buried by their uncaring Protestant parents for being possessed by witches.

It almost makes it better.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It was the best I could find.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh, well that's fun, still I don't like it for the 2 reasons before (the picture doesn't match the description and the picture in general sucks.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Every person is the first they have seen for a long, long time…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: But why does it yearn for company? It must be able to remember the people it encounters if it has a desire for people to stay.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It doesn't learn well. It's permanently stuck in that mentality.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I got that, but a child will quickly learn not to break the cats tail after the first time much like they learn not to touch fire. They either learn to stop killing people/trapping them or they know they're killing people and they do it on purpose.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You misunderstand their intent, they aren't killing on purpose. Imagine a 3 year old child's mentality. They want to play with their pets, but sometimes they don't know how much is too much. They want to be your friend, but they just do a poor job.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
They want people to spend time with them but they trap and kill them, again it's contradictory.

"We just want to be helpful! Damn, killed another one, dig another grave Johnny"
"Hey buddy, come here, we just want to tie your shoes! Fuck, another grave Johnny… Shut up Johnny, we're totally being helpful"
"Oh shit, quick get him before he leaves! NO JOHNNY! SHUT UP JOHNNY! Damn it, did you guys see that? We were so close to being helpful to that one. I swear to god Johnny, if you don't shut up I'll get Dan to dig another grave for you!"
"Oooohhh, another one, quickly guys let's help them out! Again? Really? Come on Tim, we need you to stop breaking ankles, we're helpful hands not killers. SHUT IT JOHNNY!"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The hands are clingy and pathetic. They just want someone to come into the boneyard and spend some time with them, maybe trim some grass and polish the stones or maybe not. Lets just talk about you. You will be our friend, right?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like that, but that's not being helpful, it's being clingy and pathetic. If they had that kind of understanding then they should notice that dead people aren't thankful.

However if the hands thought they were being helpful by killing people then that might change my mind.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: We are so lonely…please don't leave us….we promise to be better for you….

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
2 things made me not like this, both are petty but they tend to break most articles for me:

  • The zombie hands are made of soil but the hands in the picture are pretty clearly supposed to resemble standard zombie hands (red blood and greenish flesh.)
  • The image doesn't come across as realistic in the slightest, it looks like a scene from a low budget zombie flick and I can't take the article for anything past that.

Seriously, these are potentially helpful low-budget zombie hands that accidentally kill people and then bury them because the hands are helpful.

I also must question why the hands keep people in the cemetery when they're trying to be helpful, it's a clearly unhelpful action to block someone and kill them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: idea is spectacular, hilarious while terrifying. could use some tightening up but it's pretty good as is. upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I liked it too.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't think there's a conspiracy. I'm not blaming anyone. I'm more than willing to let people come to their own conclusions and leave it at that. I just posted in this article because it shot up in votes like a rocket compared to the last 20 or so entries and I honestly can't figure out why. Perhaps the taste of this community is just not my taste, and that's fine. But I did want to say something, and I appreciate the thoughtful responses.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ah, "as long as you are right." There's the self-obsessed, grandiose righteousness I've come to expect. Pleasing people in chat is narrow. Being big and great means knowing how to please people you don't even know yet. But as long as everyone is happy, it's not my place to criticize the end goal. I'm merely saying this path and this end goal is not my cup of tea.

I do think a lot of stuff here does qualify as "intelligent writing" as you say… but in my head there's a lot of intelligent writing here that gets bombed over petty rules and pet peeves that really don't matter. Again, it's y'alls community as you make it. Self-determination is a right. It's just not me. Be well.

edit: Trust me, I realize you can entirely come back at me about being grandiose as well with this. We have different opinions, perhaps, and I'm more than willing to be civil and just leave it there.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't mind if you call me a Drama Llama. Ultimately the consequence rests on the site, not me. I'm just an observer. For what it's worth, I hope it works out doing it this way. I like a lot of things that are here and I've been inspired a great deal. My future-goggles (wrong as they may be, your mileage on that depending on how much you trust me, the person who randomly shows up and disappears with no particular note or history, may vary) are just telling me this is a curious direction. So I spoke upon it. To each their own and best of luck!

edit: Also, being "jelly" has nothing to do with it. There are plenty of articles on this site that I wish I wrote and I commend those who wrote them. I've never even posted an SCP (I merely have a couple bare bones/incomplete things in sandbox) and I'm leaning toward not posting an SCP at any point presently. I just wanted to put in my two cents. Sorry if y'all hate my two cents :) I feel compelled not to shut up sometimes

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
At first I was all, "Ugh, zombies." And then I was like, "Oh wait, helpful zombie hands?" And then they were too helpful and I upvoted.

The last paragraph could use some work, however.

Persons will report these experiences as extremely unpleasant and will attempt to exit the cemetery as quickly as possible.

Is exiting the cemetery part of the SCP or a natural reaction? If the latter, you'd be better off phrasing the rest under the onus of "If the subject attempts to exit the cemetery".

This will continue until the person is either removed from SCP-1673 or expires.

There was something said about a time limit of 1-3 hours. What happens when that runs out?

However, if the user expires while within SCP-1673 as a result of SCP-1673's constructs, the constructs will bury the person's body.

You know, I remember something else that buries a person's body when they accidentally kill them, and how that made the article very touching in a whimsical/creepy way. That said, you could elucidate on this just a tad.

Any attempt to approach the body before it is buried will be met with hostile force.

And this, elucidate a little bit more. Just some short descriptions of how/where they bury the body, if there's a significance to the place, and what sort of force is used. "Met with hostile force" is very bland and unspecific.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It took me a second to figure out what the photo was, then I got a chill. The article built up some tension but the payoff wasn't worthwhile. Helpful hands that don't know their own strength is odd without being very interesting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I disagree; the danger feels reasonable to me. They're hands made out of dirt that lack the sensitivity to gauge necessary force. *shrug* Makes sense to me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A word of warning: It's okay to be blunt and direct, as long as you are right. If you haven't discovered it yet, there's an audience that you have to write for. And guess what? Those people are usually in the chat, post frequently on the site, and also will vote on your works. The people who know the tastes of the more active members of the site will get more upvotes. This isn't nepotism or foul-play or ass-sucking or whatever you think it is; it's called intelligent writing.

If there's anything right to what you just said, it's this: While people are entitled to their opinion, I see a new influx of users here that don't have much experience with the site, and therefore give bad advice and undeserved upvotes. That's okay; they'll learn.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: SCPs can have a backslide (Laser-pointer-car-toy), but I get feels from this one that the element of danger isn't inherent in the idea. It feels tacked-on and everything else is just meh. Local folklore, yes; SCP, doesn't feel like it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have absolutely no desire to feed the Drama Llama, but "they jelly" or some variant thereof probably applies.

For my part, I have yet to see an article get "ripped to shreds" on this site that didn't deserve it. In fact, there's been a couple lately (and no, I'm NOT naming them) that got a fair amount of upvotes that I thought were terrible. And those involved new members without the street cred that people like Roget have.

Conversely, I've seen articles get tepid and negative responses that were authored by senior staff. I dunno. IMHO, I think the community mostly does a pretty good job of rating articles around here.
